title: Autocorrelated Sampling in Cognition
text:
A fundamental question in cognitive science is how people achieve such high levels of performance given the limited resources they have access to. Human behaviour is similar to the Bayesian ideal, but implementing Bayes’ rule is intractable but for the simplest problems.
To circumvent this problem, computer scientists have developed a family of algorithms, Markov Chain Monte Carlo (Brooks et al., 2011), which operate by drawing samples directly from the posterior distribution (thus avoiding distribution multiplication), which make the inference task possible. An exciting development in cognitive science has been to show that these inference algorithms describe human behaviour remarkably well: in perception (Gershman et al., 2012), causal reasoning (Bramley et al., 2017; Davis & Rehder, 2020), forecasting (Spicer et al., 2024), numerical estimates (Lieder et al., 2018) and probability judgments (Dasgupta et al., 2017) among others 1.
In this workshop, we will:
No previous experience with MCMC algorithms is needed.
In this website, you will find a schedule for the workshop as well as workshop materials and slides.

title: Autocorrelated Sampling in Cognition
text:
Footnotes


We have compiled a list of papers using sampling approaches to cognition here↩︎

title: The samplr package
text:
Overview of the package
The samplr package is an R package available on CRAN. With it, you can:

use MCMC algorithms
use the Autocorrelated Bayesian Sampler model (Zhu et al., 2024)
calculate some useful statistics that help with model inference.








Documentation


The package documentation lives at https://lucas-castillo.github.io/samplr/

title: The samplr package
text:
Prerequisites


install.packages("samplr")




library(samplr)

title: The samplr package
text:
Metropolis Hastings















































































































































sampler_mh(
  start = 1,
  distr_name = "norm",
  distr_params = c(0, 1),
  sigma_prop = .1,
  iterations = 100
)











Documentation


You can access function documentation by prepending a function with ?, i.e. ?sampler_mh

title: The samplr package
text:
Sampling from Metropolis Hastings

Use sampler_mh() to generate samples from a Normal distribution \(\mu=3; \sigma^2=5\)
Same as above, but with a large proposal width (\(\sigma^2_{prop} = 10\)). How does the acceptance rate change?
Use sampler_mh() to generate samples from a Beta distribution \(\alpha = 4, \beta = 4\)

title: The samplr package
text:
Sampling from Metropolis Hastings (my solutions #1)

Use sampler_mh() to generate samples from a Normal distribution \(\mu=3; \sigma^2=5\)





sampler_mh(
  start = 0, 
  distr_name = "norm", 
  distr_params = c(3, sqrt(5))
)

text:

)$`Acceptance Ratio`

title: The samplr package
text:
Sampling from Metropolis Hastings (my solutions #3)
Use sampler_mh() to generate samples from a Beta distribution \(\alpha = 4, \beta = 4\)




sampler_mh(
  start = 0, 
  distr_name = "beta", 
  distr_params = c(4,4)
)






















Further Reading



See https://lucas-castillo.github.io/samplr/articles/supported-distributions.html for all supported distributions and their parameters
See https://lucas-castillo.github.io/samplr/articles/how-to-sample.html for how to sample from mixture distributions and multivariate distributions.

title: The samplr package
text:
From samples to responses – Anchoring
Lieder et al. (2018) use MCMC to provide an explanation of anchoring as resource-rational allocation of resources.



The anchoring effect
Strack & Mussweiler (1997)
How tall is the Cathedral in Cologne?


One group (high anchor):

Is the cathedral taller than 320m?
How tall is the cathedral?

Another group (low anchor):

Is the cathedral taller than 60m?
How tall is the cathedral?




Mean answers: \(\bar x_{high} = 273;\bar x_{low} = 89\)




Lieder et al. (2018)

This is because people adjust the anchor in an MCMC way
Sampling takes effort so after some time they stop (as extra sampling is more costly than estimate improvement)

title: The samplr package
text:
From samples to responses – Anchoring



Target distribution

The true height of the cathedral is 157m
Probably very uncertain \(\rightarrow \mathcal N (\mu=157, \sigma=30)\)
Let’s fix sigma_prop = 20
Start point is either start=320 or start=60
Mean answers: \(\bar x_{high} = 273;\bar x_{low} = 89\)




Replicating the effect

Make the last sample the response
How many samples are needed to replicate the effect in both groups?
Is there a situation where response=last sample might not make much sense?

title: The samplr package
section: From samples to responses – Anchoring (my solution)
text:
From samples to responses – Anchoring (my solution)




set.seed(1)
starts <- c(60, 320)
distr_name <- "norm"
distr_params <- c(157, 30)
sigma_prop <- 20

responses <- c()
for (start in starts){
  for (n in 1:200){
    responses <- c(responses, sampler_mh(
      start, 
      distr_name, 
      distr_params, 
      sigma_prop, 
      iterations = n)$Samples[n,1]
    )
  }
}















Is there a situation where response=last sample might not make much sense?


When starting point influence disappears, mean of samples is a better estimate (burn-in).

title: The samplr package
text: Sampling Functions

title: The samplr package
text:
Qualitative Features



3 qualitative features


Their inclusion or exclusion makes 8 algorithms in total (\(2^3\)).


Qualitative Features:

Multiple Chains
Gradient-based proposals
Autocorrelated proposals







In the next slides…



Overview of the features
Why they might be useful









For technical detail



See appendix of Castillo et al. (2025)
See package reference

title: The samplr package
text:
Multiple Chains


The basic idea

Have more than one chain running at the same time


First chain (cold chain) exactly like MH


Other chains have higher temperature (more likely to accept)


Sometimes chains swap positions (if the colder of the two is “happy” with the new place)


End: only consider cold chain. (in the package, take the first slice: samples[,,1])

title: The samplr package
text:
Gradient-based proposals


The basic idea

Don’t make “blind” proposals, but base them on the gradient of the target density


This increases acceptance rate without being stuck in a location.


Achieved by simulating a physical system with random momentum in each iteration. (simulate \(L\) steps of size [precision] \(\epsilon\))

title: The samplr package
text:
Autocorrelated proposals



In MH,

\(\theta^{t'} = \theta^{t-1} + n; n \sim \mathcal N (0, \sigma)\)

In Gradient-based proposals:

\(\rho \sim \mathcal N (0, 1)\)



This can lead to random-walk behaviour which is inefficient sometimes


Instead, recycle the previous jump to get a sense of trajectory.

title: The samplr package
text:
Practice
Consider the distribution



set.seed(1)
n <- 5
names <- rep("mvnorm", n)
parameters <- list()
for (i in 1:n){
  parameters[[i]] <- list(
    runif(2) * 18 - 9, 
    diag(2)
  )
}
weights <- runif(n);
weights <- weights / sum(weights)













Choose an algorithm other than MH and draw samples from the distribution.

title: The samplr package
text:
My answer



sampler_*(
  start = c(1,1), 
  distr_name = names, 
  distr_params = parameters, 
  weights = weights
)



sampler_mc3(
  start = c(1,1), 
  distr_name = names, 
  distr_params = parameters, 
  weights = weights, 
  sigma_prop = diag(2),
  nChains = 6
)$Samples[,,1]





sampler_hmc(
  start = c(1,1), 
  distr_name = names, 
  distr_params = parameters, 
  weights = weights,
  epsilon = .1, L = 10
)$Samples




sampler_mcrec(
  start = c(1,1), 
  distr_name = names, 
  distr_params = parameters, 
  weights = weights,
  epsilon = .1, L = 10,
  alpha = .5
)$Samples[,,1]

title: The samplr package
text:
References


Castillo, L., Li, Y.-X., & Sanborn, A. N. (2025). The samplr package: A tool for modeling human cognition with sampling algorithms. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/ax8hm_v2


Geyer, C. J. (1991). Markov Chain Monte Carlo Maximum Likelihood. Proc. 23rd Symposium on the Interface Interface Foundation, Fairfax Station, 1991, 156–163.


Hastings, W. K. (1970). Monte Carlo sampling methods using Markov chains and their applications. Biometrika, 57(1), 97–109. https://doi.org/10.1093/biomet/57.1.97


Lieder, F., Griffiths, T. L., M. Huys, Q. J., & Goodman, N. D. (2018). The anchoring bias reflects rational use of cognitive resources. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 25(1), 322–349. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13423-017-1286-8


Neal, R. M. (2011). MCMC Using Hamiltonian Dynamics. In S. Brooks, A. Gelman, G. Jones, & X.-L. Meng (Eds.), Handbook of Markov Chain Monte Carlo (1st ed., pp. 113–162). Chapman and Hall/CRC. https://doi.org/10.1201/b10905


Strack, F., & Mussweiler, T. (1997). Explaining the enigmatic anchoring effect: Mechanisms of selective accessibility. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 73(3), 437–446. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.73.3.437


Zhu, J.-Q., Sundh, J., Spicer, J., Chater, N., & Sanborn, A. N. (2024). The autocorrelated Bayesian sampler: A rational process for probability judgments, estimates, confidence intervals, choices, confidence judgments, and response times. Psychological Review, 131(2), 456–493. https://doi.org/10.1037/rev0000427

title: Presentation title
Workshop Prerequisites
text:
Before the workshop, please install the R programming language in your machine. An IDE such as RStudio is recommended but not necessary.
The workshop will make use of the following packages: samplr, abc; which you install by running:
install.packages(c("samplr", "abc"))
on your R console.
If you have issues installing these, please email Lucas Castillo (lucas.castillo@warwick.ac.uk).

title: People
text:
Lucas is interested in how the mind deals with its own resource limitations to achieve behaviour that is rational enough to navigate the world effectively. Using random generation, he has explored which MCMC algorithms best describe human sampling. He is the lead developer of the samplr package.







Stella is an interdisciplinary researcher incorporating her expertise on vision psychophysics, especially bistable perception and eye movements, with behavioural science and computer science. Currently, she works on developing an experimental paradigm to capture perceptual and decision making features that we can test different computational models on.







Adam Sanborn is a Professor of Psychology at the University of Warwick. Adam is interested in the rationality of human behaviour, which he studies with Bayesian models, sampling approximations to Bayesian models, and behavioural experiments.

title: The Autocorrelated Bayesian Sampler
text:
Anchoring example









ABS

title: The Autocorrelated Bayesian Sampler
text:
Many response modes


Also gives reaction time.


Figure adapted from Zhu et al. (2024)

title: The Autocorrelated Bayesian Sampler
text:
Object-Oriented Programming


Make a new instance of the model


myModel <- Zhu23ABS$new(...)




Sample from the model


myModel$simulate(...)




Look at results


myModel$sim_results

title: The Autocorrelated Bayesian Sampler
text:
Making a new instance with $new()



ABS uses MC3 as the MCMC sampler (MH + Multiple Chains)


Target distribution is Gaussian, centred at the stimulus mean.


Parameters:

width: Width of proposal distribution
n_chains: Number of Chains
distr_name: Must be "norm"
distr_params: Width of the posterior distribution.



The decision time for a trial will be \(t \sim \text{Erlang}(N, \lambda) + n.d.t.\)


\(n.d.t.\) either fixed (when s_nd_time = 0) or variable (\(n.d.t. \sim \mathcal U (nd, nd+s\_nd)\).


lambda: Parameter in Erlang distribution
nd_time
s_nd_time




myModel <- Zhu23ABS$new(
  width = 25, 
  n_chains = 6, 
  distr_name = "norm", 
  distr_params = 30, 
  lambda = 4, 
  nd_time = 1, 
  s_nd_time = 0
)

title: The Autocorrelated Bayesian Sampler
text:
Simulating results with $simulate()



For now, set stopping rule to “fixed” (I’ll explain this later!)
Sample \(N\) samples
trial_stim: Mean of the stimulus (in our example 24 - we can add multiple trials)




myModel$simulate(
  stopping_rule = "fixed", 
  n_sample=4, 
  trial_stim=c(24, 22, 27)
)

title: The Autocorrelated Bayesian Sampler
text:
Results

Examining results

print(myModel$sim_results)

  trial                   samples stimulus   rt point_est
1     1 5.21, -2.49, 11.87, -2.49       24 1.77     -2.49
2     2   4.72, 0.57, 17.19, 7.81       22  1.7      7.81
3     3 17.53, 6.37, 14.93, 30.69       27 2.39     30.69




Adding confidence intervals

myModel$confidence_interval(conf_level = .75)



  trial samples stimulus   rt point_est  ci_l  ci_u
1     1     ...       24 1.77     -2.49 -2.49  9.37
2     2     ...       22  1.7      7.81  2.13 13.67
3     3     ...       27 2.39     30.69  9.58 25.76

title: The Autocorrelated Bayesian Sampler
text:
Practice


Make ABS estimate the height of the Cologne cathedral with an anchor of 320m (the correct height is 127m).

How long does it take, when \(\lambda = 4\) and \(N_{samples} = 10\)?
What is the 80% confidence interval?



Estimate: 300
80% Confidence interval: 300-315
RT: 4.33s





model <- Zhu23ABS$new(
  width = 25, 
  n_chains = 6, 
  distr_name = "norm", 
  distr_params = 30, 
  lambda = 4, 
  nd_time = 1, 
  s_nd_time = 0
)
model$simulate(
  stopping_rule = "fixed", 
  start_point = 320, 
  n_sample=10, 
  trial_stim = 127
)
model$confidence_interval(.8)
model$sim_results

title: The Autocorrelated Bayesian Sampler
text: “Relative” Stopping Rule

title: The Autocorrelated Bayesian Sampler
text:
$simulate() with a relative stopping rule



2-alternative-forced-choice task (e.g., random dot motion task)


Model counts difference in evidence between the two hypotheses and terminates whenever it exceeds a threshold.


Parameters:

delta: threshold of the accumulated difference
dec_bdry: decision boundary that splits the posterior
prior_on_resp: the prior on responses, which determines the prior preference for a response (Beta).
discrim: discriminabilty, determines the mean values of the distributions for the posterior of hypotheses.





myModel$simulate(
  stopping_rule = "relative", 
  delta = 2, 
  dec_bdry = 0, 
  discrim = 3, 
  prior_on_resp=c(1,1),
  trial_stim = factor(
    c("left", "right", "left"))
)

title: The Autocorrelated Bayesian Sampler
text:
Practice

The speed-accuracy trade-off states that

if you’re trying to be fast, you’ll be less accurate, and
if you’re trying to be accurate, you’ll be slower



Consider a 2AFC task - how can ABS replicate this effect?

title: The Autocorrelated Bayesian Sampler
text:
My solution



Setup

set.seed(1)
stimuli <- factor(
  sample(c("left", "right"), 100, T)
)
myModel <- Zhu23ABS$new( 
  width = 2, 
  n_chains = 6, 
  distr_name = "norm", 
  distr_params = 2, 
  lambda = 4, 
  nd_time = 1, 
  s_nd_time = 0
)




Slow

slow <- myModel$simulate(
  stopping_rule = "relative", 
  delta = 10, 
  dec_bdry = 0, 
  discrim = 3, 
  prior_on_resp=c(1,1),
  trial_stim = stimuli
)$sim_results



Fast

myModel$reset_sim_results()
fast <- myModel$simulate(
  stopping_rule = "relative", 
  delta = 2, 
  dec_bdry = 0, 
  discrim = 3, 
  prior_on_resp=c(1,1),
  trial_stim = stimuli
)$sim_results

title: The Autocorrelated Bayesian Sampler
text:
My solution



mean(fast$rt);

[1] 1.627338

mean(fast$accuracy)

[1] 0.64




mean(slow$rt);

[1] 5.543572

mean(slow$accuracy)

[1] 0.95

title: The Autocorrelated Bayesian Sampler
text:
Part 1 Wrap-up
So far

Learned about MCMC and past applications to cognitive modelling
Learned about how to use the samplr package to:


produce samples from different MCMC algorithms
produce responses from the ABS cognitive framework (Zhu et al., 2024)


After the break
Learn how to fit these models to data. We need:

some data!
some simulated data from models.

title: The Autocorrelated Bayesian Sampler
text:
Experiment

Castillo et al. (2026) asked participants to say heights of people in the UK at random.


People are pretty bad at producing random sequences


Instead, they show patterns that can be used to identify which MCMC sampler best describes their item production


Instructions









Your task is to say the height of one of the people in the picture, chosen at random, every time you are prompted to do so. To do this, imagine:

Shuffling the papers in the bag
Choosing a random paper from the bag and reading the height.
Returning the paper to the bag
Repeating the process

title: The Autocorrelated Bayesian Sampler
text:
Last tasks before the break



Getting Data

Go to https://rg-exp.netlify.app/
Add a pseudonym (so that you know which is your data!)
Do a typed random generation task: 150 heights (as described before)
Send me an email with the .csv results (lucas.castillo@warwick.ac.uk)
(We’ll share everyone’s data and that’s what we’ll fit in part 2)




Simulating Data

Ask MCMC algorithms to sample from \(\mathcal N (\mu=176.4, \sigma=12)\).
Prior over models is \(1/N\).
Prior over parameters (prior.R in website).
The objective is to have a big matrix of simulated data.








Help!


If this is too much:

Example code in website to run directly
Pre-simulated data also in website (you can load it on R with myVar <- readRDS(file.rds))

title: The Autocorrelated Bayesian Sampler
text:
References


Castillo, L., León-Villagrá, P., Falbén, J. K., Chater, N., & Sanborn, A. N. (2026). Random generation is what comes to mind in naturalistic settings. Cognition, 274, 106554. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2026.106554


Zhu, J.-Q., Sundh, J., Spicer, J., Chater, N., & Sanborn, A. N. (2024). The autocorrelated Bayesian sampler: A rational process for probability judgments, estimates, confidence intervals, choices, confidence judgments, and response times. Psychological Review, 131(2), 456–493. https://doi.org/10.1037/rev0000427

title: Fitting MCMC models with Approximate Bayesian Computation
text:
The challenge
We want to come up with a posterior over models or parameters…
\[
p(\theta|D) \propto \data{rn-color=#F2D541}{\class{rn-fragment fragment}{p(D|\theta)}} P(\theta)
\] but we do not have a likelihood function.

title: Fitting MCMC models with Approximate Bayesian Computation
text:
Approximate Bayesian Computation

\[
p(\theta|D) \approx \frac 1 N \sum_{i=1}^N \delta (\mathcal M(\theta), y_{obs})\ \text{for large}\ N
\]


\(\delta(a,b): 1\ \text{if}\ a = b\ \text{else}\ 0\)


Example
Q: I rolled \(N\) 6-sided dice and got an 11. How many dice did I roll?






















































(Sisson et al., 2019; Turner & Van Zandt, 2012)

title: Fitting MCMC models with Approximate Bayesian Computation
text:
Computation-Accuracy Trade-offs



Tolerance

In the previous example, I simulated 100,000 rolls.


Only 2,895 (\(2.9\%\)) equalled 11 (throw away \(97\%\) of the data).


We can instead keep \(11 \pm \epsilon\).


For \(\epsilon = 1\), we keep \(8.68\%\) of data instead.

title: Fitting MCMC models with Approximate Bayesian Computation
text:
Computation-Accuracy Trade-offs
Summary statistics



For high-dimensional data it is difficult to be identical or very similar (e.g. random generation data is 150-dimensional).
Instead use a function \(S(\cdot)\) that reduces the dimensionality of the data
Evaluate similarity in summary space.
Ideally, the choice of summary statistics should be sufficient (very difficult in practice): \[
p(\theta|S) = p(\theta|S, y_{obs})
\]









Plan



Show you some summary statistics of your data


How close were you to true randomness?


We fit the models to the data


Which model describes your performance best?


Pointers to parameter estimation and other simulation-based approaches.

title: Fitting MCMC models with Approximate Bayesian Computation
text:
Random Generation Summary Statistics



Repetitions


Adjacencies


Turning Points


Distances


Shape


RNG


gzip

title: Fitting MCMC models with Approximate Bayesian Computation
text:
Your Results












































































































Most random




[1] "lucas"

title: Fitting MCMC models with Approximate Bayesian Computation
text:
From Summaries to Model Inference


Calculate summary statistics from the simulated data and observed data




In the website you have a summary_statistics.R file which provides the all_measures() function.


source("summary_statistics.R")


sequence <- sample(1:10, 100, T)
all_measures(sequence)

          R        A       TPF        D            S       RNG gzip
1 0.1010101 0.247191 0.5816327 3.348315 -0.002693658 0.2160881 1.01




I have also placed the data from participants today.

data <- readRDS(data.rds)
Cleaned: removed items 4SD away (e.g. “1680”) in response2

End product is a data frame for participants and models



obs_summaries[1:2,]

# A tibble: 2 × 9
  id_number pseudonym      R     A   TPF     D       S   RNG  gzip
      <dbl> <chr>      <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1      5096 lucas     0.0137 0.188 0.5    11.5 0.00155 0.151  1.30
2      5196 lks       0.0135 0.192 0.503  11.4 0.00155 0.150  1.30

title: Fitting MCMC models with Approximate Bayesian Computation
section: From Summaries to Model Inference (my solution)

title: Fitting MCMC models with Approximate Bayesian Computation
Fitting MCMC models with Approximate Bayesian Computation
text:
Inference


Select a subset of simulations where the distance between simulations and observed data is smaller than a tolerance \(\epsilon\).


Getting the mean and SD of the simulated summaries (\(M_s\), \(SD_s\))
Standardizing observed and simulated summaries with those values (\((S_{obs} - M_s) / SD_s\))
For each person, calculate Euclidean distance between each simulated row and observed summaries
For that person, look at only the closest \(10\%\) simulations.
What’s the proportion of models in that subset?


We use the abc package to do that for us.



library(abc)
fit <- abc(
  target= obs_summaries[obs_summaries$pseudonym == "lks", 3:9], 
  param = sim_summaries$models, 
  sumstat = sim_summaries[, 7:13], 
  tol = .1, 
  method = "rejection"
)




table(fit$unadj.values) / sum(table(fit$unadj.values))


   HMC    MC3  MCHMC  MCREC RECHMC RECMC3 
0.0317 0.4657 0.0898 0.0416 0.0096 0.3616

title: Fitting MCMC models with Approximate Bayesian Computation
text:
Inference
Do this for every participant then combine the likelihoods


df <- data.frame()
for (i in unique(obs_summaries$id_number)){
  fit <- abc(
    target= obs_summaries[obs_summaries$id_number == i, 3:9], 
    param = sim_summaries$models, 
    sumstat = sim_summaries[, 7:13], 
    tol = .1, method = "rejection"
  )
  result <- cbind(
    data.frame(
      id_number=i, 
      pseudonym=obs_summaries$pseudonym[obs_summaries$id_number == i]
    ),
    data.frame(table(fit$unadj.values) / sum(table(fit$unadj.values)))
  )
  df <- rbind(df, result)
}

df %>% 
  group_by(Var1) %>% 
  summarise(LL = sum(log(Freq))) %>% 
  mutate(BF = exp(LL - min(LL)))

# A tibble: 6 × 3
  Var1      LL     BF
  <fct>  <dbl>  <dbl>
1 HMC    -6.97   10.8
2 MC3    -1.52 2505. 
3 MCHMC  -4.89   86.4
4 MCREC  -6.39   19.3
5 RECHMC -9.35    1  
6 RECMC3 -2.02 1517.

title: Fitting MCMC models with Approximate Bayesian Computation
text: Inference

title: Fitting MCMC models with Approximate Bayesian Computation
text:
Parameter Estimation




library(abc)
fit <- abc(
  target= obs_summaries[
    obs_summaries$pseudonym == "lks", 3:9
  ], 
  param = sim_summaries[
    sim_summaries$models == "MC3", 
    c("sigma", "nChains")
  ], 
  sumstat = sim_summaries[
    sim_summaries$models == "MC3", 7:13
  ], 
  tol = .1, 
  method = "rejection"
)

title: Fitting MCMC models with Approximate Bayesian Computation
text:
Other Simulation-based Approaches

Do Approximate Bayesian Computation but select \(\theta\) with MCMC (use distance as ‘goodness’)



sampler_mh(..., custom_density = \(x){})



Select models via machine-learning classification (Pudlo et al., 2016)


Use a neural network to produce summary statistics (Valentin et al., 2024)


Use a neural network to classify data (e.g. Bayesflow)


A good review in Cranmer et al. (2020)

title: Fitting MCMC models with Approximate Bayesian Computation
text:
Discussion Questions

How do you see yourself using this?

What features would be useful to add for this?

title: Fitting MCMC models with Approximate Bayesian Computation
text:
References


Cranmer, K., Brehmer, J., & Louppe, G. (2020). The frontier of simulation-based inference. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 117(48), 30055–30062. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1912789117


Pudlo, P., Marin, J.-M., Estoup, A., Cornuet, J.-M., Gautier, M., & Robert, C. P. (2016). Reliable ABC model choice via random forests. Bioinformatics, 32(6), 859–866. https://doi.org/10.1093/bioinformatics/btv684


Sisson, S. A., Fan, Y., & Beaumont, M. A. (Eds.). (2019). Handbook of approximate Bayesian computation. CRC Press, Taylor & Francis Group.


Turner, B. M., & Van Zandt, T. (2012). A tutorial on approximate Bayesian computation. Journal of Mathematical Psychology, 56(2), 69–85. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jmp.2012.02.005


Valentin, S., Castillo, L., Sanborn, A. N., & Lucas, C. G. (2024). Distinguishing Between Process Models of Causal Learning. Proceedings of the Annual Meeting of the Cognitive Science Society, 46. https://escholarship.org/uc/item/2p43j2cw

Useful files
During the presentation, you will simulate synthetic random generation data. You will need:

The prior over models and parameters (download)

title: Materials
text:
Documentation to the samplr package
The samplr package is an R package available on CRAN. With it, you can:

use MCMC algorithms
use the Autocorrelated Bayesian Sampler model (Zhu et al., 2024)
calculate some useful statistics that help with model inference.

The documentation to the samplr package lives here (with some very useful vignettes!). We have also written a manuscript on how to use it and how to fit MCMC models (Castillo et al., 2025).
It is an open source package, meaning that you can look at the source code here.
If you have any feature suggestions or if you find any bugs, please report them here (or send Lucas Castillo an email).
